ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for generic savoury biscuit / cracker; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of savoury biscuits is approximately 25 g, equivalent to 4–5 standard crackers.

nutrient_source.title: Savoury Biscuit — HPB Singapore Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F11080059 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken from this source without modification.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 70 is assigned based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain salted crackers / water crackers (GI ~70, high category). Glycemic load per 25 g serving calculated as (70 × 64.22 × 0.25) / 100 = 11.24. This is consistent with the input record's embedded GI value of 70 and aligns with published GI data for refined-flour crackers.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 297 mg per 100 g — borderline range with limited potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 297 mg/100 g is in the borderline 200–400 mg range; individuals with hypertension should limit portions to 20–30 g per sitting and choose lower-sodium cracker alternatives where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic index (70) and high carbohydrate load (64.2 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (2.1 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The high GI of 70 combined with 64.2 g carbohydrate and only 2.1 g dietary fibre per 100 g produces a high glycaemic load that promotes rapid postprandial glucose elevation; people with type 2 diabetes should limit portions to 2–3 crackers and pair with protein or healthy fat to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~8.5 g/100 g and likely trans fat ~0.3 g/100 g from commercial shortening
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The estimated saturated fat content of approximately 8.5 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g threshold of concern, and the likely presence of trans fat from hydrogenated vegetable shortening makes this product unfavourable for cardiovascular health; individuals with CVD risk should choose wholegrain, low-saturated-fat crackers and verify the ingredient list for hydrogenated oils.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~8.5 g/100 g and trans fat ~0.3 g/100 g — pro-inflammatory profile
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The pro-inflammatory saturated and trans fat profile of commercial savoury biscuits is unsuitable for rheumatoid arthritis management; individuals should avoid products containing hydrogenated or partially hydrogenated vegetable oils and opt for anti-inflammatory snack alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Savoury biscuit (also known as a salted cracker or plain cracker) is a dry, crisp, baked snack product made primarily from refined wheat flour, vegetable oil (commonly palm oil or shortening), salt, and savoury seasoning. The texture is light and crunchy, with a mildly salty, neutral flavour that makes it a versatile accompaniment to cheese, dips, or spreads. It is an ultra-processed product widely consumed as a snack across Singapore and globally. Nutritionally, it is energy-dense at 481 kcal per 100 g, with a high carbohydrate content of 64.2 g/100 g dominated by refined starch, a moderate fat content of 20.9 g/100 g (with estimated saturated fat ~8.5 g/100 g from palm oil or shortening), and a relatively low protein content of 7.7 g/100 g. Dietary fibre is low at 2.1 g/100 g, and sodium is borderline at 297 mg/100 g. The glycaemic index is high at 70, producing a glycaemic load of approximately 11.2 per 25 g serving. Clinically, this food is unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes due to its high GI and low fibre, and is of concern for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis due to its saturated and likely trans fat content. It is borderline suitable for hypertension with portion control. Data sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo Food Composition Database (cr_id F11080059).
common_user_queries: How many calories in a savoury biscuit?
Is salted cracker good for hypertension?
Are plain crackers high in sodium?
Can I eat crackers with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Input record sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent. GI value of 70 is corroborated by University of Sydney GI Database data for plain salted crackers.
notes: iron_mg is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source for this record; this is a confirmed zero per the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (cr_id F11080059) — iron content is negligible or below detection threshold for this refined-flour cracker product. saturated_fat_g of 8.5 g/100 g is estimated from the total fat of 20.87 g/100 g based on typical palm oil / shortening composition in commercial savoury biscuits; the HPB source does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. trans_fat_g of 0.3 g/100 g is estimated based on typical commercial shortening usage; not directly reported by HPB. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this generic savoury biscuit (cr_id F11080059) does not report these values, and no supplementary approved source (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, USDA Foundation Foods) provides a sufficiently close match to justify substitution without introducing estimation error: monounsaturated_fat_g — detailed fatty acid fractionation not reported by HPB for this entry; polyunsaturated_fat_g — detailed fatty acid fractionation not reported by HPB for this entry; potassium_mg — mineral not reported in HPB entry for this record; cholesterol_mg — not reported by HPB for this entry; vitamin_c_mg — not reported by HPB for this entry, and negligible in refined-flour baked products; vitamin_a_mcg_rae — not reported by HPB for this entry; vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg — not reported by HPB for this entry; vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg — not reported by HPB for this entry; vitamin_b3_niacin_mg — not reported by HPB for this entry; vitamin_b6_mg — not reported by HPB for this entry; vitamin_b12_mcg — not reported by HPB for this entry; folate_mcg_dfe — not reported by HPB for this entry; vitamin_d_mcg — not reported by HPB for this entry; vitamin_e_mg — not reported by HPB for this entry; phosphorus_mg — not reported by HPB for this entry; magnesium_mg — not reported by HPB for this entry; zinc_mg — not reported by HPB for this entry; selenium_mcg — not reported by HPB for this entry; water_g — moisture content not reported by HPB for this entry. Source consulted and confirmed unavailability: HPB Singapore Foodo (https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/, accessed 2026-04-16). This food is not a Singaporean dish and therefore multilingual naming requirements (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) do not apply; it is a generic Western-style snack product with global distribution.
